name: San Francisco, California
tagline: A consolidated city-and-county of ~870K residents running a ~$15.9B budget under Mayor Daniel Lurie. Enterprise IT runs through the Department of Technology (DT/SFTech) under the City CIO; SF is an early, governance-forward generative-AI adopter that put Microsoft 365 Copilot in front of ~30,000 city workers and is modernizing under real fiscal pressure.
overview: The City and County of San Francisco is a consolidated city-county — a single government of roughly 870,000 residents that performs both municipal and county functions — led by an elected Mayor and an 11-member Board of Supervisors. Enterprise technology runs through the Department of Technology (DT, also branded SFTech), the city's centralized IT provider: it is led by the City Chief Information Officer, who also serves as DT's Executive Director, reports roughly 260 employees and an operating budget near $140 million, and delivers infrastructure, cloud, networking, public-safety radio, broadband, and cybersecurity to more than 30,000 city employees across dozens of departments. The CIO reports to the Mayor with functional reporting to the City Administrator. The defining context for the current cycle is fiscal stress under Mayor Daniel Lurie, who took office in January 2025: the city adopted a roughly $15.9 billion two-year budget (FY2025-26 / FY2026-27) that closed an ~$800 million deficit, ordered hundreds of millions in further cuts mid-year, and faces additional structural gaps and federal-funding risk in the out-years. Despite austerity, San Francisco is one of the most prominent municipal AI adopters in the country — it published citywide Generative AI Guidelines and rolled Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat out to nearly 30,000 employees. For a Dell SLG seller this is a large, sophisticated municipal opportunity, but one where the message must fit a cost-pressured, efficiency-first environment and route through the Office of Contract Administration and Chapter 21 purchasing rules.

budget.intro: San Francisco adopted a roughly $15.9 billion FY2025-26 budget (with the two-year plan running through FY2026-27) that closed an initially projected ~$800 million deficit, and Mayor Lurie ordered additional cuts during the year as the outlook worsened. This is an austerity cycle: out-year deficits are projected to keep growing and federal-funding cuts add risk. Technology spend is not one concentrated line — DT holds the enterprise IT appropriation (operating budget near $140M), but large departments (Public Health, MTA, the airport, public safety) carry their own substantial IT.

stats.label: FY2025-26 city budget
stats.note: Two-year plan through FY2026-27; closed an ~$800M deficit

stats.label: DT operating budget
stats.note: Department of Technology; ~260 employees serving 30,000+ city staff

stats.label: Opening deficit closed
stats.note: Plus mid-year cuts; larger structural gaps projected in out-years

notes: The current cycle is a cost-pressure year — the ~$800M gap was closed with cuts and the Mayor ordered further reductions mid-year, which favors efficiency, consolidation, and automation pitches over net-new capital.

Technology budgets are distributed: DT owns enterprise IT and shared infrastructure, but large departments (DPH, SFMTA, SFO/Airport, public safety) fund and run their own systems — verify the owning department before forecasting.
The Mayor's Office and the Controller publish the authoritative budget and update letters; confirm current figures against those sources before sizing a deal, given mid-year restraint.

initiatives.name: Citywide Microsoft 365 Copilot rollout
initiatives.summary: After a ~2,000-employee, six-month pilot, San Francisco made Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat available to nearly 30,000 city employees at no additional cost under its existing Microsoft license — one of the largest local-government generative-AI deployments anywhere. A live lane for AI-infrastructure, data-readiness, endpoint, and security conversations, with a comparable municipal reference valued over decks.

initiatives.name: Generative AI Guidelines & AI governance
initiatives.summary: DT's Emerging Technologies team, with the city's AI Advisory Committee, maintains citywide Generative AI Guidelines (updated July 2025) built on transparency, no-deepfakes, and data-protection principles, with a risk-tiered approach and a tool inventory. The city has signaled a fuller citywide AI Strategy. Governance-forward posture means AI and data plays should lead with security, privacy, and compliance fit.

initiatives.name: Cloud & infrastructure modernization (SFCloud)
initiatives.summary: DT runs SFCloud as a primary cloud option for city storage and compute and stood up a Cloud Center of Excellence (2023) to guide cloud adoption and migration across departments. An ongoing modernization track touching hybrid cloud, storage, data protection, and managed services — and a natural fit for ISG and as-a-service positioning.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity operations & broadband equity
initiatives.summary: DT's Office of Cybersecurity defends citywide systems under the City CISO, while the Fiber to Housing / broadband-equity programs extend connectivity to first responders and underserved communities. Security operations, identity, endpoint, and resilience are active spend areas, and connectivity programs add a network/infrastructure angle.

procurement.intro: Selling to San Francisco runs through the Office of Contract Administration (OCA, the city's Purchasing Division) under the City Administrator, governed by Chapter 21 of the Administrative Code — not a single buying desk. OCA administers hundreds of citywide term contracts for goods, services, and technology that departments buy from, and larger or sole-source deals carry additional approval steps. Vendors must register and meet city contractor-compliance requirements. Confirm the owning department first, then map the OCA/Chapter 21 path and any usable cooperative contract.

vehicles.name: Office of Contract Administration (OCA) — citywide term contracts
vehicles.detail: OCA, the Purchasing Division, runs competitive solicitations and maintains citywide term contracts (including technology) that departments must check before buying. The central path for catalog-style and citywide agreements under Chapter 21.

vehicles.name: Chapter 21 purchasing rules & approvals
vehicles.detail: Chapter 21 of the Administrative Code governs how departments buy goods and general/professional services, including thresholds, competition requirements, and approvals — larger or non-competitive awards face added scrutiny. Factor these gates into deal timing.

vehicles.name: Cooperative purchasing contracts
vehicles.detail: The city can leverage cooperative vehicles to extend pre-competed pricing and terms where a usable co-op exists — often faster than a stand-alone city solicitation. Confirm which co-ops Dell holds and which SF actively buys through for a given category.

vehicles.name: Contractor compliance & vendor registration
vehicles.detail: Vendors must register and meet San Francisco's contractor-compliance requirements (e.g., equal-benefits, local-business, and related programs) before award. Clear these early so they don't stall an otherwise-ready deal.

powerStructure.name: Daniel Lurie
powerStructure.title: Mayor
powerStructure.note: Took office January 2025; the city's chief executive who owns budget priorities, the deficit-closing cuts, and the high-profile citywide AI push. The executive context for any enterprise-scale technology program.

powerStructure.name: Carmen Chu
powerStructure.title: City Administrator
powerStructure.note: Reappointed by Mayor Lurie (Dec 2025); oversees 25+ departments including the Office of Contract Administration, and is the functional reporting line for the City CIO. A key relationship for procurement and shared-services strategy.

powerStructure.name: Michael (Mike) Makstman
powerStructure.title: City CIO & Executive Director, Department of Technology
powerStructure.note: Permanent CIO after serving interim from Dec 2023; a former city CISO. Leads DT, the AI rollout, and cloud modernization — the primary enterprise-IT relationship for the city motion. Verify current status before forecasting.

powerStructure.name: City Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.title: Office of Cybersecurity, Department of Technology
powerStructure.note: Appointed by the CIO in consultation with the City Administrator; leads citywide security policy and the Cyber Defense Division. The security buying-center owner; confirm the current name before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Board of Supervisors (11 members)
powerStructure.title: Governing body / budget & contract approval
powerStructure.note: Approves the budget and larger contracts through its committees (e.g., Budget & Finance). Board and committee timing gates any sizable technology deal.

news.title: Mayor Lurie signs balanced two-year budget closing an ~$800M deficit
news.impact: Confirms austerity and tighter scrutiny on new spend — frame infrastructure, consolidation, and automation as savings enablers, not added cost.

news.title: PermitSF portal goes live on OpenGov as part of Lurie's permitting overhaul
news.impact: A flagship digital-services and data-modernization initiative (and a contracting one) — signals appetite for platform modernization while the OpenGov contract draws scrutiny on cost and delivery.

news.title: Mayor Lurie orders ~$400M in additional city budget cuts amid worsening outlook
news.impact: Reinforces an efficiency-first posture and out-year fiscal risk — lead with cost-takeout and as-a-service over net-new capital.

news.title: San Francisco rolls out Microsoft 365 Copilot to nearly 30,000 city workers; updates GenAI Guidelines
news.impact: Workforce-wide AI adoption plus refreshed governance — an opening for AI-infrastructure, data-readiness, endpoint, and security conversations grounded in compliance fit.

news.title: Mayor Lurie reappoints Carmen Chu as City Administrator
news.impact: Continuity in the office that oversees procurement (OCA) and the CIO's functional reporting line — a stable relationship map for the city motion.

dellActions: Anchor the account on the Department of Technology under City CIO Mike Makstman as the enterprise buying center, while separately mapping large departments (DPH, SFMTA, SFO, public safety) that run their own IT budgets and procurement.

Lead with efficiency and consolidation in an austerity year: position ISG, infrastructure refresh, and managed services as cost-takeout against a budget that just closed an ~$800M deficit and ordered further mid-year cuts — not added spend.

Tie AI-infrastructure and data-readiness to the citywide Microsoft 365 Copilot rollout and SF's Generative AI Guidelines, leading with security, privacy, and governance fit and a strong comparable municipal reference.

Engage the Office of Cybersecurity (City CISO) on security operations, identity, endpoint, and resilience, and pair cloud modernization (SFCloud / Cloud Center of Excellence) conversations with as-a-service economics.

Get the procurement path right early: confirm vendor registration and contractor-compliance requirements, identify the owning department, and route through OCA citywide term contracts, Chapter 21 rules, or a usable cooperative contract — factoring Board/committee timing for sizable deals.
